title: Definable Quotient Spaces for Unlikely Intersection Problems
speaker: Thomas Scanlon
abstract: Applications of o-minimality to unlikely intersection problems usually begin with the observation that the relevant analytic covering maps are definable.  However, this observation is almost never literally true in that the maps are definable on a suitable fundamental set, not on the full domain of definition, and this non-definabily is often leveraged in functional transcendence proofs.   Various formalisms to describe this situation have been introduced by, for example, Zilber, Bakker-Klingler-Tsimerman, and Eterović-Scanlon.  I will discuss a common refinement of these formalism and how it can be used to deduce automatic uniformity statements.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: O-minimal definability as controlled analytic geometry
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates definable quotient spaces by recalling that many analytic maps are not globally definable, defining structures and o-minimality, and emphasizing the tools supplied by o-minimality such as tame topology, point counting, and definable Chow.

proof_stages.label: Definable double quotients via fundamental sets
proof_stages.summary: For a definable group G, definable subgroup M, and nondefinable discrete group Gamma, the quotient Gamma\G/M is made definable by choosing a definable fundamental set, and morphisms are required to respect these chosen domains up to finitely many translates.

proof_stages.label: Complex definable quotient spaces and previous applications
proof_stages.summary: Adding algebraic and complex-analytic compatibility to the quotient data yields complex definable quotient spaces, from which one obtains algebraic differential equations and transfer principles for likely and unlikely intersection results.

proof_stages.label: Dependence of quotient structure on the fundamental domain
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the definable structure on a quotient is not intrinsic to the abstract quotient but depends on the chosen fundamental domain, a fact reflected in examples where different domains yield field-like, group-like, or trivial analytic structures.

proof_stages.label: Zilber-Castle trichotomy as the model-theoretic target
proof_stages.summary: Zilber's analysis of bi-algebraic structures and Castle's theorem suggest that finite-Morley-rank structures built from complex or o-minimal data should fall into a trichotomy: field, modular group, or disintegrated.

proof_stages.label: Projection and finite-rank questions for analytic or special relations
proof_stages.summary: For a complex definable quotient, the key structural question becomes whether definable analytic or special relations are closed under projections in a proper-mapping-like way, which would imply finite Morley rank and hence the trichotomy.

proof_stages.label: Jordan Brown's coset-structure theorem for the trivial discrete quotient
proof_stages.summary: In the simplified case with no discrete quotient, Jordan Brown proves that the structure whose basic relations are all cosets of definable subgroups in powers of a real group satisfies the Zilber-style conclusion: it cannot recover an ordered or field structure, only possible modular abelian group structure.
